data.title: Consumer Fears Of Inflation Spike After Iran War, University Of Michigan Says
data.description:
The University of Michigan's preliminary consumer sentiment reading for March 2026 came in at 55.5, a modest 1.9% decline from February's 56.6 and the lowest level in three months.

• United States Oil Fund stock is showing upward bias. Where is USO stock headed?

But the headline number masks a much sharper deterioration beneath the surface: roughly half of the survey interviews were conducted before U.S. military action in Iran began, and half afterward.

The split reveals a very different story.

Interviews completed after the strikes began came in sharply lower and with rising inflation concerns — a divergence that Surveys of Consumers Director Joanne Hsu said was driven almost entirely by the Iran conflict.

Gas Price Shock Arrives in the Data

“Prior to the conflict, sentiment had been improving. Then, as soon as that conflict began, all the interviews thereafter came in lower — same thing with gas price expectations,” Hsu said during an interview on Bloomberg.

Gasoline prices are where the Iran conflict registered most sharply. In the first half of the survey window — Feb. 17 through 28 — one-year gas price increase expectations stood at 10%.

In the nine days that followed the start of U.S. military operations, they surged to 42.6%. Five-year expectations moved from 27.1% to 49.2% over the same period.

Oil prices — as tracked by the United States Oil Fund (NYSE:USO) — returned above $95 a barrel as of Friday morning, marking a 40% increase since the start of the war.

The AAA national average gasoline price stands at $3.63 per gallon, a sharp increase from $2.94 a month ago, underscoring the rapid spike in fuel costs following the escalation of the Iran conflict.

The shift is already feeding into consumer inflation expectations.

Among respondents interviewed after the conflict began, year-ahead inflation expectations climbed from 3.3% to 3.5%, while long-run expectations rose from 3.1% to 3.3% — an early sign that rising gasoline prices may be reshaping the inflation outlook.

Survey Split: Before vs. After Iran Conflict Began

Personal Finances, Jobs, and a 7.5% Confidence Drop

The damage extended beyond energy prices. Expectations for personal finances fell 7.5% nationally month over month — a broad-based decline that Hsu said cut across income, age and political affiliation.

Labor market views continued a year-long deterioration, with consumers broadly expecting unemployment to rise and reporting elevated probabilities of losing their own jobs.

More striking, according to Hsu, is who led the decline in the second half of the survey: the highest-income, highest-wealth consumers — the cohort whose robust spending propped up aggregate consumption in 2023 and 2024 — also posted sharp sentiment declines after the conflict began.

Not 2022 — But the Risk Is Different Now

When asked to compare the current consumer mood to the period around Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in 2022, Hsu noted that sentiment levels are not dramatically different. The key distinction, she argued, is the macro context.

Before the Russian invasion of Ukraine, inflation had already been on a steep upward trajectory, approaching its June 2022 peak. Consumers were absorbing a surging cost of living with limited relief in sight.

For this crisis, the situation is different. Inflation had been decelerating for years.

But the labor market is now weaker, and the geopolitical shock has hit at a moment when the spending cushion from high-income consumers may be eroding.

"Consumers expect their purchasing power to be eroded in the year ahead," Hsu said.

Photo: hxdbzxy via Shutterstock

data.title: Kroger Shares Rise As Pharmacies Add Eli Lilly's Zepbound KwikPen
data.description:
The Kroger Co. (NYSE:KR) shares are up on Friday as the company expands access to high-demand weight management medication.

The company confirmed the launch of Zepbound KwikPen at participating Kroger pharmacies. The Zepbound KwikPen is a multi-dose delivery option for Eli Lilly and Company’s (NYSE:LLY) GLP-1 therapy, now available for self-pay patients.

Kroger’s stock performance is moving in tandem with the overall market trends, with the consumer discretionary sector gaining 0.21% today. This aligns with

KR Technical Analysis

The stock is currently trading 7.2% above its 20-day simple moving average (SMA) and 14.5% above its 100-day SMA, demonstrating strong short-term momentum.

Over the past 12 months, shares have increased by 13.44% and are positioned closer to their 52-week highs than to their lows.

The RSI is at 66.72, which is considered neutral territory, while the MACD shows a bullish signal with a value of 1.9724, above its signal line at 1.5882. This combination suggests that while the stock is nearing overbought territory, the bullish momentum remains intact.

  • Key Resistance: $76.50
  • Key Support: $61.00

Kroger is currently underperforming its sector, with a daily gain of 0.13% compared to the Consumer Discretionary sector’s increase of 0.21%. The sector ranks 8 out of 11, reflecting mid-tier performance, and has seen a decline of 5.15% over the past 30 days, indicating a challenging environment for consumer discretionary stocks.

Despite the current underperformance, Kroger’s recent initiatives may help bolster its position within the sector. The company's focus on expanding access to affordable healthcare solutions aligns well with evolving consumer demands, which could provide a competitive edge moving forward.

The recent launch of Zepbound KwikPen at Kroger pharmacies highlights the company’s commitment to making healthcare more accessible and affordable. This strategic move not only enhances Kroger’s service offerings but also positions the company favorably in a competitive retail landscape.

Earnings & Analyst Outlook

Kroger is slated to provide its next financial update on June 22, 2026 (estimated).

  • EPS Estimate: $1.59 (Up from $1.49)
  • Revenue Estimate: $45.39 Billion (Up from $45.12 Billion)
  • Valuation: P/E of 48.7x (Indicates premium valuation)

Analyst Consensus & Recent Actions: The stock carries a Buy Rating with an average price target of $74.07. Recent analyst moves include:

  • Evercore ISI Group: Outperform (Raises Target to $83.00) (Mar. 9)
  • Citigroup: Neutral (Raises Target to $71.00) (Mar. 9)
  • Roth Capital: Buy (Raises Target to $78.00) (Mar. 6)

Top ETF Exposure

Significance: Because KR carries such a heavy weight in these funds, any significant inflows or outflows for these ETFs will likely force automatic buying or selling of the stock.

KR Stock Price Activity: Kroger shares were up 0.17% at $75.09 at the time of publication on Friday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

data.title: A Well-Paid TV And Film Crew Member Says The Industry Is Dead. After A Year Of Unemployment, They Say LA Is Impossible Without LA Money
data.description:
For years, working in television and film in Los Angeles meant steady union pay and a comfortable middle-class life for many crew members. But one veteran worker now says that reality has vanished.

After more than a year without work, the 55-year-old film professional recently shared their situation online, saying the industry they spent more than two decades building a career in now feels “dead.” They said the financial math of living in Los Angeles simply doesn’t work anymore without the high wages that production jobs once provided.

A Career That Once Paid More Than $3,000 A Week

The crew member explained that it took years to climb into a well-paid position on film and television sets. Once they did, the income was strong thanks to union protections.

“When I worked my way into my crew position, I was shocked how much I was making (strong union) over +3k a week,” they wrote on Reddit’s r/MiddleClassFinance recently.

Don't Miss:

That income helped them buy their first home using a U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs loan. But the situation changed dramatically after the Hollywood actor and writer strikes in 2023 and a broader slowdown in production.

The worker says they managed to survive financially for a while by relying on savings, but after more than a year of unemployment, the future feels uncertain.

“I've survived but it's time to move on to something new but I don't know what or how,” they wrote. “I'm stuck and my accounts are dwindling and I can't just wait until it's dire.”

Their biggest realization is that Los Angeles, one of the most expensive cities in the U.S., only works financially with the kind of paychecks film crews once relied on.

“I can't live in LA w/out LA money,” they wrote.

Industry Slowdown Hits Crew Workers Hard

Other workers in the discussion said the problem isn’t limited to one person. Many described a widespread downturn in production jobs across the entertainment industry.

Several commenters said the boom years fueled by streaming platforms have cooled. “When all the streamers were first starting up, they were producing massive amounts of content,” the commenter wrote. “Now that the dust is settling, they’ve cut down a bunch on how much they’re making.”

Trending: Don't risk buyer's remorse — ask these critical questions every homebuyer should know.

Others pointed to different factors contributing to the slowdown, including productions moving overseas, declining theater attendance, and the aftermath of the writers’ and actors’ strikes.

“Whole industry is contracting,” one person wrote. “Marvel pulled out of Atlanta, leaving a lot [of] crew out of work. As much as it seems LA is bad LA is actually still doing better than most other US production centers.”

For mid-level crew workers who once earned solid incomes but don’t have large financial cushions, the downturn has created difficult decisions.

“[High cost of living] areas are not for the marginally employed or long-term unemployed,” another commenter wrote.

Considering A New Life Outside Hollywood

Facing uncertain prospects in the industry, the original poster said they’re starting to consider leaving Los Angeles entirely.

Other commenters encouraged that idea, arguing that the high cost of living makes staying risky without steady production work.

“If you don’t have anything keeping you here, why stay in L.A.,” one person wrote. “Don’t wait until you are really struggling and literally cannot afford to relocate.”

The OP seemed open to the possibility of a major life change. At one point, they said they were seriously considering a much simpler job.

“I do kinda lust for a part-time job in a hardware store,” they wrote.

The comment reflected a broader theme throughout the discussion: many film workers are quietly reassessing their careers as the industry shifts.

The Hollywood career that once delivered a stable middle-class income may not return.

Image: Shutterstock
